name: Creation
summary: God creates the heavens and the earth, forming an ordered world and making humanity in his image. The seventh day is blessed as the pattern of divine rest.
longDescription:
God creates the heavens and the earth, forming an ordered world and making humanity in his image. The seventh day is blessed as the pattern of divine rest.

The biblical text (Genesis 1:1-2:3) places creation in the setting of Garden of Eden and connects it with God, Adam, Eve. The record keeps to the details supplied by Scripture: the action, the response of faith or rebellion, and the way the event moves the covenant story forward.

Within Primeval History, this event helps explain the next stage of the timeline by showing God’s judgment, provision, promise, or leadership at a specific point in the biblical story. Later biblical writers often return to these themes when they interpret creation, covenant, exodus, conquest, worship, faith, and kingship.
historicalNotes: Scholarly opinion: Genesis 1 uses ordered literary structure that contrasts sharply with polytheistic ancient Near Eastern creation stories by presenting one sovereign Creator.

name: The Fall
summary: Adam and Eve disobey God in Eden and are exiled from the garden. The chapter explains the entrance of sin, shame, death, and hope into the human story.
longDescription:

name: Cain and Abel
summary: Cain murders his brother Abel after God regards Abel and his offering. God judges Cain, yet also marks him so vengeance will not consume him.

summary: Genesis traces the line from Seth to Noah, preserving the family through which the later flood narrative will move. The genealogy emphasizes both human mortality and God’s continuing purposes.

name: The Flood

historicalNotes: Archaeological evidence: Flood traditions are known from Mesopotamian texts such as Atrahasis and the Gilgamesh Epic, though Genesis gives the event a distinct moral and covenantal interpretation.

name: Noahic Covenant
summary: God blesses Noah’s family, renews the command to fill the earth, and establishes a covenant with all flesh. The rainbow is given as the sign that God will not again destroy all flesh by flood.

historicalNotes: Archaeological evidence: Babel is commonly associated with Babylon, where ziggurat temple towers were prominent in Mesopotamian urban religion.

Within The Patriarchs, this event helps explain the next stage of the timeline by showing God’s judgment, provision, promise, or leadership at a specific point in the biblical story. Later biblical writers often return to these themes when they interpret creation, covenant, exodus, conquest, worship, faith, and kingship.
historicalNotes: Historical reconstruction: The patriarchal narratives fit a second-millennium BC setting of clan migration, pastoral life, treaties, and movement between Mesopotamia, Canaan, and Egypt.

longDescription:

historicalNotes: Historical reconstruction: Egypt was a natural refuge during Levantine famine because the Nile system often provided food when Canaan suffered drought.

name: Joseph Rules Egypt

The biblical text (Genesis 41:37-57) places joseph rules egypt in the setting of Egypt and connects it with Joseph, God, Jacob. The record keeps to the details supplied by Scripture: the action, the response of faith or rebellion, and the way the event moves the covenant story forward.

historicalNotes: Historical reconstruction: The Joseph story reflects known Egyptian administrative concerns with grain storage, famine management, and royal officials, though Joseph himself is not securely identified in Egyptian records.

name: Jacob Moves to Egypt
summary: Jacob’s family moves to Egypt after Joseph reveals himself and provides for them during famine. God assures Jacob that he should not fear going down to Egypt and that God will bring his family up again.
longDescription:

Within The Patriarchs, this event helps explain the next stage of the timeline by showing God’s judgment, provision, promise, or leadership at a specific point in the biblical story. Later biblical writers often return to these themes when they interpret creation, covenant, exodus, conquest, worship, faith, and kingship.
historicalNotes: Historical reconstruction: Semitic groups are well attested in Egypt during the second millennium BC, especially in Delta contexts, though the biblical family migration cannot be independently verified by name.

name: Deaths of Jacob and Joseph
summary: Jacob blesses his sons and is buried in Canaan, while Joseph reassures his brothers that God meant their evil for good. Joseph dies in Egypt, still trusting God’s promise to bring Israel back to the land.
longDescription:

name: Israel Enslaved in Egypt

historicalNotes: Historical reconstruction: The eastern Nile Delta is the likely background for Goshen and store-city labor; Egyptian texts attest forced labor, though they do not name Moses.

longDescription:

name: Moses Flees to Midian

The biblical text (Exodus 3-4) places the burning bush in the setting of Mount Sinai, Midian, Egypt and connects it with Moses, God, Aaron, Jethro. historicalNotes: Scholarly opinion: The traditional identification of Mount Sinai with Jebel Musa is ancient but not certain; other Sinai and northwest Arabian locations have been proposed.

name: The Ten Plagues
summary: Through Moses and Aaron, God strikes Egypt with ten plagues that expose Pharaoh’s resistance and display divine power. The judgments distinguish Israel from Egypt and lead toward the Passover.
longDescription:
Through Moses and Aaron, God strikes Egypt with ten plagues that expose Pharaoh’s resistance and display divine power. The judgments distinguish Israel from Egypt and lead toward the Passover.

The biblical text (Exodus 7-11) places the ten plagues in the setting of Egypt, Goshen and connects it with Moses, Aaron, God, Miriam. The record keeps to the details supplied by Scripture: the action, the response of faith or rebellion, and the way the event moves the covenant story forward.

Within Exodus & Wilderness, this event helps explain the next stage of the timeline by showing God’s judgment, provision, promise, or leadership at a specific point in the biblical story. Later biblical writers often return to these themes when they interpret creation, covenant, exodus, conquest, worship, faith, and kingship.
historicalNotes: Scholarly opinion: Some interpreters seek naturalistic Egyptian plague sequences, while the biblical text presents them primarily as divine judgments against Pharaoh and Egypt’s gods.

name: The First Passover
summary: Israel marks its doors with the blood of the Passover lamb while God judges Egypt’s firstborn. The night becomes a lasting memorial of deliverance from slavery.
longDescription:
Israel marks its doors with the blood of the Passover lamb while God judges Egypt’s firstborn. The night becomes a lasting memorial of deliverance from slavery.

The biblical text (Exodus 12) places the first passover in the setting of Egypt, Goshen and connects it with Moses, Aaron, God, Miriam. The record keeps to the details supplied by Scripture: the action, the response of faith or rebellion, and the way the event moves the covenant story forward.

Later biblical writers often return to these themes when they interpret creation, covenant, exodus, conquest, worship, faith, and kingship.
historicalNotes: Historical reconstruction: Passover is presented as Israel’s founding deliverance meal, later embedded in Israel’s annual worship calendar. Scholarly opinion: The traditional early Exodus date is approx. 1446 BC, while a late-date alternative places the Exodus in the thirteenth century BC.

name: The Red Sea Crossing
summary: Pharaoh pursues Israel after the exodus, trapping them by the sea. God parts the waters for Israel and brings the sea back over the Egyptian army.
longDescription:
Pharaoh pursues Israel after the exodus, trapping them by the sea. God parts the waters for Israel and brings the sea back over the Egyptian army.

The biblical text (Exodus 14) places the red sea crossing in the setting of Egypt and connects it with Moses, God, Miriam, Aaron. The record keeps to the details supplied by Scripture: the action, the response of faith or rebellion, and the way the event moves the covenant story forward.

Within Exodus & Wilderness, this event helps explain the next stage of the timeline by showing God’s judgment, provision, promise, or leadership at a specific point in the biblical story. Later biblical writers often return to these themes when they interpret creation, covenant, exodus, conquest, worship, faith, and kingship.
historicalNotes: Scholarly opinion: The Hebrew phrase can be rendered Sea of Reeds, and proposed crossing locations vary; the text emphasizes deliverance rather than providing a modern site marker. Scholarly opinion: The Exodus is often dated by an early chronology to approx. 1446 BC, while a late-date alternative places it in the thirteenth century BC.

name: Manna and Quail
summary: In the wilderness, Israel grumbles for food, and God provides quail and daily manna. The provision teaches Israel to depend on God and prepares them for Sabbath obedience.
longDescription:

The biblical text (Exodus 16) places manna and quail in the setting of Mount Sinai and connects it with Moses, Aaron, God, Miriam. The record keeps to the details supplied by Scripture: the action, the response of faith or rebellion, and the way the event moves the covenant story forward.

name: The Ten Commandments

The biblical text (Exodus 19-20) places the ten commandments in the setting of Mount Sinai and connects it with Moses, God, Aaron, Miriam. The record keeps to the details supplied by Scripture: the action, the response of faith or rebellion, and the way the event moves the covenant story forward.

Later biblical writers often return to these themes when they interpret creation, covenant, exodus, conquest, worship, faith, and kingship.
historicalNotes: Archaeological evidence: Ancient Near Eastern treaty forms provide useful background for covenant structure, though Israel’s covenant is framed as revelation from the Lord at Sinai.

name: The Golden Calf
summary: While Moses is on the mountain, Israel makes a golden calf and worships it. Moses intercedes for the people, judges the sin, and the covenant crisis reveals both Israel’s rebellion and God’s mercy.
longDescription:

The biblical text (Exodus 32) places the golden calf in the setting of Mount Sinai and connects it with Aaron, Moses, God, Miriam. The record keeps to the details supplied by Scripture: the action, the response of faith or rebellion, and the way the event moves the covenant story forward.

name: The Tabernacle Built
summary: Israel brings offerings and skilled work for the tabernacle exactly as God commanded Moses. When the work is completed, the glory of the Lord fills the tabernacle.

The biblical text (Exodus 35-40) places the tabernacle built in the setting of Mount Sinai and connects it with Moses, Aaron, God, Miriam. The record keeps to the details supplied by Scripture: the action, the response of faith or rebellion, and the way the event moves the covenant story forward.

Later biblical writers often return to these themes when they interpret creation, covenant, exodus, conquest, worship, faith, and kingship.
historicalNotes: Historical reconstruction: Portable shrines and tent sanctuaries are known in ancient Near Eastern settings, but the tabernacle’s meaning is governed by the biblical worship instructions.

The biblical text (Numbers 13-14) places the twelve spies in the setting of Kadesh-barnea, Canaan and connects it with Moses, Joshua, Caleb, Aaron, Miriam. historicalNotes: Historical reconstruction: Kadesh-barnea is usually placed in the Negev/Sinai border region, but exact identification remains debated.

name: Balaam

longDescription:

historicalNotes: Archaeological evidence: The Deir Alla inscription mentions a seer named Balaam son of Beor, showing that a Balaam tradition was known in the region, though the inscription is not the biblical text itself.

name: Death of Moses
summary: Moses views the promised land from Moab but dies before entering it. The text honors him as the prophet whom the Lord knew face to face and points to Joshua as his successor.
longDescription:
Moses views the promised land from Moab but dies before entering it. The text honors him as the prophet whom the Lord knew face to face and points to Joshua as his successor.

The biblical text (Deuteronomy 34) places death of moses in the setting of Moab and connects it with Moses, God, Joshua, Aaron, Miriam. The record keeps to the details supplied by Scripture: the action, the response of faith or rebellion, and the way the event moves the covenant story forward.

Later biblical writers often return to these themes when they interpret creation, covenant, exodus, conquest, worship, faith, and kingship.
historicalNotes: Historical reconstruction: Mount Nebo in Moab is the traditional region associated with Moses viewing the land, though Deuteronomy says his burial place was unknown.

name: Joshua Commissioned
summary: After Moses’ death, God commissions Joshua to lead Israel across the Jordan. Joshua is commanded to be strong and courageous and to meditate on the law.
longDescription:

The biblical text (Joshua 1) places joshua commissioned in the setting of Jordan River, Canaan and connects it with Joshua, God, Moses, Caleb. Later biblical writers often return to these themes when they interpret creation, covenant, exodus, conquest, worship, faith, and kingship.
historicalNotes: Historical reconstruction: The early-date chronology places the conquest around 1406 BC; a late-date Exodus model moves related events into the thirteenth century BC.

name: Crossing the Jordan
summary: The priests carry the ark into the Jordan, and the waters stop so Israel can cross on dry ground. Twelve stones are set up as a memorial at Gilgal.
longDescription:

The biblical text (Joshua 3-4) places crossing the jordan in the setting of Jordan River, Gilgal and connects it with Joshua, God, Caleb. The record keeps to the details supplied by Scripture: the action, the response of faith or rebellion, and the way the event moves the covenant story forward.

name: Fall of Jericho

longDescription:

Later biblical writers often return to these themes when they interpret creation, covenant, exodus, conquest, worship, faith, and kingship.
historicalNotes: Archaeological evidence: Jericho is a real ancient city with complex destruction layers; scholars debate how those layers relate to Joshua 6 and to early versus late conquest chronologies.

name: The Sun Stands Still
summary: When southern kings attack Gibeon, Joshua leads Israel in battle and asks God for extended daylight. The narrative emphasizes that the Lord fought for Israel.

historicalNotes: Historical reconstruction: The allotment lists preserve ancient territorial memory, though matching every boundary marker to modern geography is difficult.

name: Joshua's Farewell

name: Cycle of the Judges
summary: After Joshua’s generation dies, Israel repeatedly turns to other gods, suffers oppression, cries out, and receives deliverers. Judges 2 summarizes the pattern that frames the book.

After Joshua’s generation dies, Israel repeatedly turns to other gods, suffers oppression, cries out, and receives deliverers. Judges 2 summarizes the pattern that frames the book.

The biblical text (Judges 2:6-23) places cycle of the judges in the setting of Shiloh, Canaan and connects it with God, Joshua. The record keeps to the details supplied by Scripture: the action, the response of faith or rebellion, and the way the event moves the covenant story forward.

historicalNotes: Historical reconstruction: Judges depicts decentralized tribal life before monarchy, matching a period when Israel did not have a central royal administration.

name: Deborah and Barak

name: Gideon

historicalNotes: Historical reconstruction: Bethlehem and Moab are real locations, and the story’s levirate and redemption themes fit Israelite family and land concerns.

name: Samuel Called
summary: Hannah prays for a son and gives Samuel to the Lord’s service at Shiloh. God calls Samuel as a prophet in a time when Eli’s house is under judgment.

name: Ark Captured and Returned
summary: The Philistines capture the ark after defeating Israel, and Eli dies when he hears the news. God afflicts the Philistines, and they return the ark with offerings, showing that Israel’s God is not defeated.
longDescription:
The Philistines capture the ark after defeating Israel, and Eli dies when he hears the news. God afflicts the Philistines, and they return the ark with offerings, showing that Israel’s God is not defeated.

historicalNotes: Historical reconstruction: Philistine city-states were major coastal powers in the Iron Age, providing the setting for Israel’s conflicts in 1 Samuel.
